---
title: "Corpus Ingestion"
guide-section: "Operations"
---

# Corpus Ingestion

The RAG system answers from a searchable knowledge base built out of Oregon
housing-law documents. This chapter explains how those documents become a Vertex
AI Search datastore. For the exact command invocations, see the
[Command Reference](08-command-reference.qmd).

::: {#fig-ingestion-pipeline}
```{mermaid}
graph TD
    subgraph "Document sources"
        ORS[Oregon Revised<br/>Statutes]
        Portland[Portland City<br/>Codes]
        Eugene[Eugene City<br/>Codes]
    end

    subgraph "Pipeline"
        ASCII[enforce-ascii]
        Meta[generate-metadata]
        Upload[upload-to-gcs]
        Store[create-datastore-gcs]
        App[create-app-gcs]
    end

    subgraph "Storage"
        Bucket[GCS Bucket]
        Datastore[Vertex AI Search<br/>Datastore]
    end

    ORS --> ASCII
    Portland --> ASCII
    Eugene --> ASCII
    ASCII --> Meta
    Meta --> Upload
    Upload --> Bucket
    Bucket --> Store
    Store --> Datastore
    Datastore --> App
```

The corpus ingestion pipeline, from source documents to a queryable datastore.
:::

## Source documents

Legal documents are stored as **pure-ASCII** text files under
`backend/scripts/documents/`, organized by jurisdiction and year:

- State laws — `documents/or/<year>/*.txt` (e.g. `documents/or/2025/ORS090.txt`).
- City codes — `documents/or/<city>/<year>/*.txt`
  (e.g. `documents/or/portland/2025/PCC30-01.txt`).

::: {.callout-note}
The ASCII requirement is enforced by the pipeline; see the corpus-dataset
contributor rules for the rationale.
:::

## Pipeline stages

The five stages run in order. Each is a `mise run` task (full flags in the
[Command Reference](08-command-reference.qmd)).

1. **Enforce ASCII** (`enforce-ascii`) — walks the documents tree and rewrites
   known non-ASCII offenders, rejecting anything it cannot fix. Run with
   `-- --check` to validate without mutating.
2. **Generate metadata** (`generate-metadata`) — infers jurisdiction from the
   directory structure and writes `metadata.jsonl`, mapping each file to its GCS
   URI and its city/state attributes. Scope flags (e.g. `-- --portland`) rewrite
   the file for that scope only.
3. **Upload to GCS** (`upload-to-gcs`) — creates a fresh bucket (it fails if the
   bucket already exists, so each ingestion gets a clean dedicated bucket) and
   uploads every file referenced by `metadata.jsonl`, plus `metadata.jsonl`
   itself, flat at the bucket root.
4. **Create datastore** (`create-datastore-gcs`) — creates a Vertex AI Search
   datastore pointing at the bucket and triggers an import from `metadata.jsonl`,
   which attaches the city/state metadata used for jurisdiction-filtered
   retrieval. The script prints the datastore ID on completion.
5. **Create app** (`create-app-gcs`) — creates a Vertex AI Search app linked to
   the datastore, for browsing/previewing it in the GCP console. The backend's
   `VertexAISearchRetriever` queries the datastore directly and does not depend on
   this app.

::: {.callout-tip}
After the datastore is created, set `VERTEX_AI_DATASTORE_LAWS` in `.env` to its
ID so the backend retrieves against the new corpus (see
[Configuration & Logging](06-configuration.qmd)).
:::

## Where to go next

- [Command Reference](08-command-reference.qmd) — every task and its flags.
